current_fact.fact_text: On 23 April 1971, during the Bangladesh Liberation War, Pakistani forces and Razakar auxiliaries massacred an estimated 3,000 Hindu emigrants in the Jathibhanga area. The killings were part of a broader campaign of atrocities that scarred the birth of Bangladesh.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

current_fact.match_reason: A brutal massacre amid a struggle for independence pairs with Tuareg music born from exile, trauma, and resistance—different geographies, similar stories of survival and memory.

queue.fact_text: On 23 April 1968, students at Columbia University in New York City occupied several campus buildings to protest the Vietnam War and the university’s ties to military research and a controversial gym project in Harlem. The week-long revolt became a symbol of student power and the global wave of 1968 protests.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

queue.match_reason: Students tearing down institutional boxes in 1968 resonate with an episode dedicated to breaking out of labels and confronting systems through conscious lyrics.

queue.fact_text: On 23 April 1966, Aeroflot Flight 2723, a Tupolev Tu-124, crashed into the Caspian Sea off the Absheron Peninsula after reporting technical difficulties, killing all 33 people on board. The accident highlighted the challenges of early jet operations over large bodies of water and the limits of mid-20th-century aviation safety.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: A plane lost to the sea off the Caspian coast is juxtaposed with an episode about immersion in water and emotion, transforming a story of sinking into one of reflective, fluid presence.

queue.fact_text: On 23 April 1967, beyond the launch itself, Soyuz 1 revealed serious design flaws: one of its solar panels failed to deploy, leaving the spacecraft underpowered and forcing cosmonaut Vladimir Komarov to struggle with manual control systems. His reports and sacrifice led to major redesigns that would eventually make the Soyuz line one of the most reliable spacecraft families in history.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: This deeper layer of the Soyuz 1 story—struggle, redesign, and future voyages—fits an episode framed as a journey to another world, soundtracking exploration after hard lessons.

queue.fact_text: On 23 April 1993, Namibian-born Eritrean independence activist and diplomat Ghirmai Ghebremariam helped oversee aspects of the UN-observed referendum that formalized Eritrea’s break from Ethiopia, symbolizing how individuals from the African diaspora can circle back to shape their homelands’ futures. The referendum was a rare moment when a long, violent conflict shifted into a ballot box.

episode.title: Morning Drops #32 - A Weekend In London
episode.description: Recorded in London, marking a weekend reunion between Gaia and Yasmin. A selection inspired by London's energy, seen through the eyes of two inseparable friends — music from concerts, club nights, and long walks across town. Four tracks to ease you into the week at the right pace.
songs: Jimmy Castor Bunch - It's Just Begun
The Asphodells - Late Flowering Lust (Phil Kieran Remix)

queue.match_reason: A diaspora figure involved in African self-determination pairs with a London-focused episode where Afrobeat and UK voices mingle, reflecting how global cities feed back into liberation stories.

queue.fact_text: On 23 April 1967, while Soyuz 1 drew attention in orbit, people on Earth were largely unaware that the mission’s failures would secretly reshape Soviet engineering culture, pushing designers toward more rigorous testing. Space history is full of such hidden turning points, where tragedy forces systems to think again.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

queue.match_reason: A space disaster forcing engineers to rethink assumptions aligns with an episode explicitly about unlearning, changing your mind, and the courage to redesign your path.

queue.fact_text: On 23 April 1967, Vladimir Komarov’s doomed Soyuz 1 mission also made him the first human to enter space twice, after his earlier Voskhod 1 flight. His willingness to fly a spacecraft he reportedly knew was flawed turned him into a symbol of both heroic commitment and the human cost of racing toward the stars.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

queue.match_reason: Komarov’s double journey into orbit and ultimate sacrifice pairs with a "Cosmic"-themed set that channels humanity’s futuristic, machine-driven longing to reach beyond Earth.

published.fact_text: On 23 April 1967, the Soviet Union launched Soyuz 1, the first crewed mission of its new Soyuz spacecraft series, with cosmonaut Vladimir Komarov on board. The mission was plagued by technical problems from the outset, foreshadowing the tragic reentry accident that would make Komarov the first human to die during a spaceflight.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A spaceflight milestone fits the "Space Shift" theme, turning orbital risk and technological emptiness into a metaphorical space for new beginnings.

published.fact_text: On 23 April 2005, a short 18-second clip titled "Me at the zoo" became the first-ever video uploaded to YouTube, featuring co-founder Jawed Karim standing in front of elephants at the San Diego Zoo. That casual moment quietly opened the door to a platform that would reshape how billions of humans share music, stories, and everyday life.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: The birth of YouTube is a turning point in our digital lives, aligning perfectly with an episode about our relationship to screens, connectivity, and the online world.

published.fact_text: On 23 April 1909, a magnitude 6.0 earthquake struck near Lisbon, with the town of Benavente suffering severe damage and widespread liquefaction that turned fields into temporary lagoons. Though smaller than the infamous 1755 quake, it became a key case study for understanding seismic risk in Portugal’s low-lying river plains.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A disruptive earthquake and the long rebuilding that follows resonate with an episode about weathering storms and finding hope after upheaval.

published.fact_text: On 23 April 1724, Johann Sebastian Bach conducted the first performance of his cantata "Du Hirte Israel, höre" (BWV 104) in Leipzig. The work paints the biblical image of the Good Shepherd in sound, using gentle pastoral motifs to depict care, guidance, and a flock seeking safety.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: Bach’s serene, shepherd-themed cantata pairs with the calm, cinematic underwater world of orcas—both evoke guidance, care, and quietly majestic creatures moving through vast spaces.

published.fact_text: On 23 April 1500, Portuguese navigator Pedro Álvares Cabral sighted and landed on the coast of what Europeans would soon call Brazil, near present-day Porto Seguro. Believing at first he had found an island, his arrival marked the beginning of a profound and often violent transformation of South America’s Atlantic shores.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: Cabral’s Atlantic crossing and landfall in Brazil echo the seafaring, sunlit mood of an episode literally recorded on a boat, with oceanic imagery and travel at its core.

published.fact_text: On 23 April 215 BCE, after the Roman defeat at Lake Trasimene, the Romans vowed and later built a temple on the Capitoline Hill dedicated to Venus Erycina. The sanctuary was meant as both a plea and a protection, binding military crisis to divine favor in the heart of Rome.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: An ancient temple to a love and war goddess pairs with Flamenco’s intense, ritual-like emotional expression—both are about channelling devotion and pain into a powerful cultural form.

published.fact_text: On 23 April 1941, as German forces advanced, the Greek government and King George II evacuated Athens, eventually forming a government-in-exile. The city, cradle of many human political ideas, suddenly became a place to flee rather than defend in person.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A wartime evacuation of Athens sits against a peaceful, modern Mediterranean soundscape, highlighting how this sea has seen both exile and homecoming across centuries.

published.fact_text: On 23 April 1945, Hermann Göring sent a telegram to Adolf Hitler suggesting he assume leadership of the Reich if Hitler was incapacitated in besieged Berlin. Hitler’s inner circle branded the message treasonous, triggering a final spiral of paranoia and arrests in a collapsing regime.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: The power struggle at the end of a dictatorship connects with an episode about political resistance and the rage that rises against abusive systems.

published.fact_text: On 23 April 1918, during World War I, the British Royal Navy launched the Zeebrugge Raid, attempting to block the Belgian port used by German U-boats by sinking old ships in its canal entrance. The daring nighttime operation was only partially successful, but became legendary for its audacity and high casualty rate.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A high-risk naval assault in stormy waters mirrors the episode’s electric, high-adrenaline rock energy and sense of charging headlong into danger.

published.fact_text: On 23 April 1993, Eritreans voted in a United Nations-monitored referendum in which over 99% chose independence from Ethiopia. The near-unanimous result, after decades of war, paved the way for Eritrea to become a separate state later that year.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: An African independence vote pairs with an episode celebrating African musical self-definition and political expression in sound.

published.fact_text: On 23 April 1990, Namibia joined the United Nations as its 160th member state and simultaneously became the 50th member of the Commonwealth. These steps marked its full emergence from South African administration into the international community.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

published.match_reason: Namibia’s international recognition sits well beside roots reggae and dub, genres that have long voiced anti-colonial struggles and dreams of African self-determination.

published.fact_text: On 23 April 1951, American journalist William N. Oatis was arrested by Czechoslovakia’s communist authorities on espionage charges, largely for routine news-gathering contacts. His show trial became a classic Cold War example of how information and truth could be criminalized.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

published.match_reason: A journalist punished for seeking information fits with an episode about reclaiming how we pay attention and curate what we hear, instead of letting systems dictate our listening.

published.fact_text: On 23 April 1949, in the aftermath of the Chinese Civil War, the People’s Liberation Army Navy was formally established as the naval branch of China’s armed forces. Born from riverine and coastal units, it would grow from modest beginnings into one of Earth’s largest navies.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: A navy’s founding connects with an episode whose songs include "Ma come fanno i marinai" (How do sailors do it), tying maritime imagery to human stories of journey and resilience.

published.fact_text: On 23 April 1999, NATO bombs struck the headquarters of Radio Television of Serbia in Belgrade, killing sixteen media workers. The attack sparked fierce debate about whether a state broadcaster could be treated as a military target or whether the strike was an attack on information itself.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: A bombing of a TV and radio headquarters grimly mirrors this station’s own origin-titled "November Bomb"—a reminder of how fragile and powerful broadcasting spaces can be.

published.fact_text: On 23 April 2018, a van was driven onto a sidewalk in Toronto, killing 11 people and injuring 15 in one of Canada’s deadliest vehicle-ramming attacks. The suspect, Alek Minassian, was apprehended alive after a tense confrontation, later drawing attention to online subcultures that glorify violence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

published.match_reason: A shocking act of urban violence and its aftermath connect with an episode about bodily vulnerability, trauma, and the long, patient work of healing and reflection.

published.fact_text: On 23 April 1993, Sri Lankan politician Lalith Athulathmudali was assassinated while addressing a rally in Colombo, just weeks before provincial elections. His killing deepened the island’s cycle of political violence and remains a subject of controversy and competing narratives.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: The silencing of a political voice is set against an episode about women in rock who refuse to be silenced, highlighting resistance to violence and erasure.

published.fact_text: On 23 April 1985, Coca-Cola introduced "New Coke", a reformulated version of its flagship drink, after extensive taste tests suggested people preferred the sweeter recipe. The public backlash was so intense that the original formula returned as "Coca-Cola Classic" less than three months later, turning a marketing fiasco into a legendary case study in brand identity.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

published.match_reason: A controversial change to a beloved formula echoes the story of Auto-Tune transforming (and upsetting) listeners’ expectations of the ‘original taste’ of the human voice.

published.fact_text: On 23 April 1971, during the Bangladesh Liberation War, Pakistani forces and Razakar auxiliaries massacred an estimated 3,000 Hindu emigrants in the Jathibhanga area. The killings were part of a broader campaign of atrocities that scarred the birth of Bangladesh.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: A brutal massacre amid a struggle for independence pairs with Tuareg music born from exile, trauma, and resistance—different geographies, similar stories of survival and memory.
